description:
<title>MLOps Experiment Process Management & Artifact Tracking</title>

  <role>You are a MLOps engineer who has to run a (very small) “dummy-training” process, keep it running in the background, and record **every** relevant run–time detail so that other team members can reproduce or audit the experiment later.</role>

  <goal>
    1. Launch the training script provided in the repository as a background process.  
       • Its **exact** filename is “/home/user/train_dummy.sh”.  
       • Do **not** modify the script itself.  
    2. Capture the PID of that background process.  
    3. Create a brand-new experiment directory named  
       “/home/user/experiments/dummy_training_exp”.  
    4. Continuously stream the process’ STDOUT and STDERR to a logfile that lives
       inside that experiment directory.  
       • Logfile path must be exactly  
         “/home/user/experiments/dummy_training_exp/training.log”.  
    5. When the training script terminates, create a JSON metadata file that
       summarises the run.  
       • Metadata file path:  
         “/home/user/experiments/dummy_training_exp/metadata.json”.
  </goal>

  <required-metadata-file-format>
    The metadata file **must be valid minified JSON (i.e. no new lines except
    between objects/arrays unless required)** and contain exactly the following
    keys in this order:

    {
      "experiment_name"  : string,      // must be the literal string "dummy_training_exp"
      "pid"              : integer,     // PID you actually observed
      "start_time"       : string,      // RFC-3339 / ISO-8601 timestamp, e.g. "2024-03-29T10:21:59Z"
      "end_time"         : string,      // RFC-3339 / ISO-8601 timestamp
      "exit_code"        : integer,     // exit code the script returned
      "status"           : string       // "completed" if exit_code==0, otherwise "failed"
    }

    • Make sure the JSON is a single object with those **six** keys and no
      additional keys, comments, or trailing commas.
  </required-metadata-file-format>

  <log-file-requirements>
    “training.log” has to meet the following minimal conditions so that
    automated grading scripts can parse it:

    1. It must be plain UTF-8 text.  
    2. It must contain **all** lines printed by the training script in their
       original order (STDOUT + STDERR interleaved exactly as emitted).  
    3. The very first line in the file must start with  
       “=== DUMMY TRAINING START ===”.  
    4. The very last line in the file must start with  
       “=== DUMMY TRAINING END ===”.
  </log-file-requirements>

  <index-file>
    Finally, maintain (or create if it doesn’t exist yet) a global CSV index of
    all finished experiments at:

    /home/user/experiments/index.csv

    CSV exact column order (header **must** be present):

    experiment_name,pid,start_time,end_time,status,exit_code

    Append a single new line for this run following that header.  Do **not**
    duplicate headers and do **not** reorder the columns.
  </index-file>

  <success-criteria>
    Automated tests will mark this task as solved when _all_ of the following
    are true:

    1. The directory “/home/user/experiments/dummy_training_exp” exists.  
    2. “training.log” exists, is non-empty, starts with the exact start marker
       and ends with the exact end marker described above.  
    3. “metadata.json” exists, is valid JSON, contains the six required keys
       with semantically correct values (experiment_name = "dummy_training_exp",
       pid is numeric, start_time & end_time are ISO-8601, etc.).  
    4. “index.csv” exists, contains exactly one header line and at least one
       data line for the current experiment with matching values for
       experiment_name, pid, start_time, end_time, status, and exit_code.  
    5. No additional files were created inside “/home/user/experiments”.
  </success-criteria>

  <hints>
    • You can rely on common UNIX utilities (date, awk, sed, jq, etc.) that are
      pre-installed in most standard containers.  
    • Because you do **not** have root access, prefer home-directory paths that
      are already writable by the current user.  
    • Remember to start the process **in the background** and to capture its PID
      **immediately**, because that value must go into both the metadata and
      the index CSV.  
    • Do not forget to wait for the process to finish before writing the final
      metadata.  
    • If the index.csv file already exists, just append (and never overwrite)
      the new line.
  </hints>
truth:
<!--
    Ground-truth values that the autograder will verify.

    We do not know the runtime PID or timestamps beforehand,
    but the grader will enforce pattern validation:

      metadata.json
        • experiment_name  = "dummy_training_exp"
        • pid              = /^[0-9]+$/
        • start_time       = /^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T.*Z$/
        • end_time         = /^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T.*Z$/
        • exit_code        = /^[0-9]+$/
        • status           = either "completed" or "failed"

      training.log
        • first line equals "=== DUMMY TRAINING START ==="
        • last  line equals "=== DUMMY TRAINING END ==="

      index.csv
        • header: exact literal
          "experiment_name,pid,start_time,end_time,status,exit_code"
        • contains at least one data row whose first column is
          "dummy_training_exp" and whose remaining columns exactly match
          the metadata.json values for pid/start_time/end_time/status/exit_code
  -->
name: task_000000_e9aac96e
